summary: Retract the ten seam root exports to nothing — the payload is internal by ruling, and the root face must stop naming its rows, its compiler, and its version

edit.description: Retract all ten. Off `./declarations.js` (84-94): the type block `AssemblyFactRow`/`ClauseRow`/`Declarations`/`EdgePlacements`/`KindFactRow`/`RequirementRow`/`SatisfiesRow`, and the value line `SEAM_VERSION`/`compileDeclarations`. Off `./emit.js` (97): `PayloadMember` out of the `export type` list — `EmitResult`/`RegistrationFact`/`ResolveOptions`/`SettingsResidue` and `emit` stay. Delete the 'Declaration rows — the erased program the seam carries' section comment (83) with its block; no subpath home, no re-export elsewhere. The module header (1-14) narrates `emit` compiling to the JSON pipe and stays true — leave it unless the retraction makes a sentence false. `declarations.ts` keeps every symbol exported module-side (its own 42-48 block, `EdgePlacements` 485, `compileDeclarations` 719, `SEAM_VERSION` 743): this retracts the ROOT face only, not the internal one.

Two sites, not one — the inbox note named only the second. The value import off `../src/index.js` carries `compileDeclarations` (12), and the type import (25) carries `ClauseRow`/`RequirementRow` alongside `Predicate`/`Requirement`, which are root nouns and stay. Retarget the retracted names to `../src/declarations.js`, keeping `clause`/`count`/`harness`/… and `Predicate`/`Requirement` on the root — the SDK's own suite reaching past the public face is the honest spelling of a spec-internal seam, not a workaround.

Third retarget site: `compileDeclarations` in the root value import (22, off `../src/index.js` at 35) moves to `../src/declarations.js`; the ~20 bare call sites are unaffected. Then extend the existing removed-exports test (623-628) — it already walks `import * as sdk` (36) for names the root must not carry (`decision`/`law`/`bound`/`genre`/`genreValue`) — with the retraction's two value names, so the value half is pinned where a re-export cannot silently restore it. Extend that test; never add a second one beside it. The eight type-only names erase at runtime and are unreachable from that walk — `root_exports.test.ts`'s `.d.ts` walk plus strict `tsc` is what holds them.

The closure walk's generated-family exclusion, re-pointed at the ruled boundary. Its header (10-14) justifies the exclusion by the OPEN `(seam-rows-public-face)` fork and names `Declarations`/`PayloadMember` as root-exported aliases — both claims retire with this entry: the fork is ruled, and neither name is root-exported after it. Re-word to the ruling: the row family is internal, versioned in lockstep, and reaches the face only by inference through `EmitResult` — nameability was never the boundary, the never-made stability promise is. The `continue` skipping generated-declared exports (88) goes dead the same moment — no root export is declared under `generated/` once `Declarations`/`PayloadMember` retract; delete it rather than leave a guard for a case that cannot arise. `isAuthoringNoun`'s own `generated/` filter (77) stays live and load-bearing: `EmitResult` still NAMES both types (emit.ts:653/655), and that filter is what keeps the walk from calling them holes.

tests.asserts: the root namespace carries neither `compileDeclarations` nor `SEAM_VERSION` — the value half of the retraction pinned against a silent re-export
the closure still holds over the retracted face: every type a root-exported declaration names is root-exported, the generated row family excluded by declaration file

acceptance: The root face names no seam row type, no `EdgePlacements`, no `SEAM_VERSION`, no `compileDeclarations`, no `PayloadMember`; the rows reach a caller only by inference through `emit()`'s return (pipeline.md, "Emit": the payload is internal, versioned in lockstep, not a designed public interchange; one is admitted when a consumer exists, and none does). `pnpm --dir sdk test` green (strict `tsc` + `node --test`); `cargo test` green.
notes: Ruled 2026-07-16; scoped at 0c25b2c. Re-verified on disk: zero consumers outside sdk/test — `.temper/harness.ts` imports `emit`/`harness`, both READMEs the same, `docs/proposals/` none, no Rust fixture spells a retracted name; the engine mirrors `SEAM_VERSION` independently (src/drift.rs:512) and is untouched. The inbox undercounted the retarget: three sites, not one.

summary: `mention-reachable` joins the closed predicate vocabulary: one generic variant taking the source's scope field and the target's gate field as arguments, judged where mentions already resolve

edit.description: The variant and its wire. `Predicate` (81) grows `mention-reachable`; `GlobValid { field }` (246, doc 240-245) is the field-argument model, but this is the FIRST predicate taking TWO field arguments — source scope and target gate — and that is what does not fit the existing shape. Grow every exhaustive match: `predicate_from_row` (296; the `glob-valid` arm 351) decodes the wire key, `key()` (427) returns `"mention-reachable"`, `target()` (459-489) and `documented_field()` (498-527) both chain single-`Some(field)` arms (466/505) that two arguments cannot join — the set predicates' `None` arms are the standing precedent for a predicate naming no one schema property. `ranges_over_selection` (441-451, non-exhaustive) is the routing question, not a free choice: 0028 makes the clause each-grain over selected members, yet its judge needs the mention graph, and `Degree` (445) is the standing precedent for exactly that — an each-grain predicate whose judge lives in `graph.rs` and whose `decide` arm returns `Indeterminate`.

The lock column for the second argument. `ClauseRow` (2431-2485) carries one `field` column (2442) plus one per-predicate argument column each (`count` 2456, `target` 2461, `degree` 2464, `bound`, `charset`, `keys`, `values`, `range`, `section`). A two-field predicate needs its own new `Option` column beside them — `field` alone cannot carry both ends — with its TOML write/read in the row's `to_table`/`from_table` neighbours. The row is the wire the SDK and engine share, so the column name is the seam's vocabulary.

Admissibility and the node-scope match. `decide` (590) already takes `all: &[Features]` alongside the member's own — the cross-member context this predicate needs is already threaded, so no new plumbing is owed here. Its exhaustive match (from 595; the `GlobValid` arm 704-722) must grow; the selection-scoped `Indeterminate` fallthrough (823-828) is where the variant lands if the judge lives in `graph.rs` per `Degree`'s precedent. Admissibility's three non-exhaustive gates take the variant as their bar allows: `judgeless` (197-206), `bodyless` (174-191, the body-feature fence at `Locus::Embedded`), `vacuities` (212-290) — whether an empty glob set on either argument is a vacuity is this entry's call to make and state.

The exhaustive match at 110-124 breaks the build on any new variant — this is a compile-time forcing function, not an optional edit. `mention-reachable` constrains no single frontmatter property (it reads the TARGET member's field across the graph), so it emits no JSON-Schema keyword: the no-op arm that `GlobValid` already sits in at 123. Keystroke validation is decidable predicates over one document's properties, and this predicate is neither — the schema channel is honestly silent here, not gapped.

The judge's home — every input is already in scope. `declared_globs(member, field)` (576) is the exact helper that reads a member's glob field; `dead_registration` (553-589) is the standing conservative posture, and its comment at 576-577 states it — a glob `globset` cannot compile is TREATED AS MATCHING so the gate never cries wolf — the same leniency direction 0028's advisory severity encodes, and the precedent to follow rather than reinvent. The `Degree` clause path (245-258) already receives `selections`, `edges`, `mention_edges`, and `by_kind` together, and `resolved_mention_edges` (881-890) resolves a mention to its target member. **The trigger is the target's declared gate FIELD carrying a non-empty value — never a registration lookup.** 0028's phrase 'whose target member registers by `paths-match`' does not select skills on this tree: `Registration::PathsMatch` (kind.rs:544) is carried by the `rule` kind alone (builtin_lock.toml:80), while `skill` registration is `user-invoked` + `description-trigger` (builtin_lock.toml:88, builtins.ts:181) and its `paths` is documented, cited, and probe-verified as a GATE that deliberately adds no paths-match entry (builtins.ts:122-131). Encoding that phrase literally ships a clause that can never fire on the rule-to-skill mention the whole ruling exists for. Implement the field-argument trigger 0028's own design sentence states ('source scope field and target gate field are arguments, like `glob-valid`'s field — and hard-codes no kind'); containment is literal per the declared leniency — every source glob appears verbatim in the target's gate.

The authoring factory. `mentionReachable(scopeField, gateField)` beside `globValid` (116) in the node-scope factory run (59-116). `Predicate` (23-46) is an open bag of optional slots (`key`, `field`, `args`, `target`, `charset`, `keys`, `values`, `range`, `section`), not a discriminated union — the second field argument needs a slot, named to match the lock column `drift.rs` gains.

The factory joins the root export block (26-51 — alphabetical, `globValid` at 35; `Predicate` itself already type-exports at 25, so the closure walk in `sdk/test/root_exports.test.ts` stays green with no edit). This one file is the whole reason this entry serializes behind SEAM-EXPORTS-RETRACT rather than running beside it: that entry retracts ten names from this same export face.

The lowering to the row. `clauseRow` (65-110) copies `predicate.key` and `predicate.field` unconditionally (68-69), so the first field argument needs no change; the SECOND needs its own column ternary beside the existing per-predicate ones. `nodeScopeBoundArgs` (115) is the neighbouring shape. This is the SDK half of the seam whose Rust half is `drift.rs`'s new column — the two must spell one name.
Regenerated from `src/drift.rs`'s `ClauseRow` by ts-rs, never hand-edited — it moves because the row gains a column. `tests/seam_bindings_current.rs` is the gate that catches a stale binding.

tests.asserts: both diagnoses fire and the silent cases stay silent: a scoped source whose globs are not literally contained in the mentioned target's gate is an advisory finding; an unscoped source mentioning a gated target is the second; a contained source, an ungated target, and a target the mention does not reach are each no finding
a `mention-reachable` clause round-trips through the lock's new argument column with both field ends intact — the seam the SDK writes and the engine reads spell one name

acceptance: The vocabulary carries `mention-reachable`: a clause naming it loads and judges, an unknown predicate still rejects at load, both diagnoses fire, and literal containment is the declared leniency (contract.md, "clause": the predicate vocabulary is closed, the enum in code is the authority, and adding one is a deliberate language change). No default contract adopts it yet — that is MENTION-REACHABLE-RULE-CLAUSE. `cargo test` green; `pnpm --dir sdk test` green.
notes: Derived from 0028 (14a803b). Collision surfaced, NOT encoded — the full statement rides the src/graph.rs description: 0028's "registers by `paths-match`" selects rules on disk, never skills, so read literally the ruling's own rule-to-skill case never fires. The trigger is the target's gate field, per 0028's own design sentence. scoped at d19124a.

summary: The `rule` default contract adopts `mention-reachable` at advisory over `paths` to a skill's `paths`, and the frozen built-in lock re-derives — a bare harness covered with zero opt-in

edit.description: `ruleDefaultContract` (647-666) gains the clause. Its three existing entries are the spelling model, each `clause(predicate, { severity, guidance, cite })`: `forbiddenKeys` (648), `globValid("paths")` (654), `maxLines(200)` (660). Severity is **advisory** by the ruling, never taste — 0028 fixes it because literal containment false-fires on a semantically contained narrower glob. Guidance carries what the predicate cannot decide: the leniency, and the two remedies the ruling names (scope the source to the gate, or ungate the target). The `cite` is an external fact and gets the full ritual per builtins.md — re-fetch the live page and re-date it at build time; never copy a neighbour's date forward. The standing gating cite to start from is code.claude.com/docs/en/skills (retrieved 2026-07-15, verified 2.1.210) at the skill `paths` doc (129-130); 0028's hard-error and gating behavior are probe-verified on 2.1.211, 2026-07-16. If the live page no longer carries the gating sentence, that is a finding to surface, not a cite to soften.

Regenerated whole, never hand-patched: the `rule` kind's clause rows (258-284, where `glob-valid` on `paths` sits at 265-270) gain the new row. The file's own header (1-20) declares it derived from the memberless emit. `tests/builtin_lock_frozen.rs` (the byte-compare at 77) re-runs the SDK module's own emit through a real `node` subprocess and fails loud on any hand-edit, so the only honest motion here is to re-derive.

The rule default contract's own suite — it grows the coverage for the adopted clause. Existing tests here pin the three shipped clauses; the fourth joins them, including that its severity is advisory (an escalation would be the corpus's declared act, never the default's — intent.md invariant 5).

tests.asserts: the rule default contract carries `mention-reachable` at advisory with guidance and a cite; a rule whose `paths` do not sit inside a mentioned skill's gate produces an advisory finding and never an error, so the gate does not block on a check that can be wrong
the embedded built-in lock still byte-equals the SDK module's own memberless emit with the new clause row in it — the lock is derived, never transcribed

acceptance: A bare harness is covered with zero opt-in: `rule`'s default contract carries `mention-reachable` at advisory, guided and cited to a re-verified live source, and the embedded lock byte-equals the SDK's own memberless emit (builtins.md, "The clauses live in code": every shipped clause is decidable, severity-declared, guided, cited and dated). `cargo test` green; `pnpm --dir sdk test` green.
notes: Derived from 0028 (14a803b), whose Rejected list settles the two live objections. Opt-in-only adoption is rejected — the author who makes this mistake is the one who never opts in — so it ships in the default contract; engine-side always-on is rejected because a known false alarm needs an off switch, which is what makes it a clause. A corpus that hits the false alarm retunes or drops one clause in its own contract. scoped at d19124a.

summary: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` cites a memory doc that now reads four hops: the gate constant and its own source disagree, and the doc sentence cannot settle which counting is temper's

gate.reason: A human probe rules the hop semantics — the doc text cannot. Fetched live at 420da04 (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-16): 'Imported files can recursively import other files, with a maximum depth of four hops.' Both readings survive that sentence: the root's own import is hop 1 and the chain caps at four edges (⇒ 4), or 'imported files ... recursively import' names a recursion four deep ATOP that first import (⇒ 5, today's constant). Only measuring the runtime's actual load depth decides it, the headless-testbed act that produced the `(mention-gate-containment)` evidence — never plan's to guess and never build's to fetch, because guessing here edits a live gate's semantics: a cap too low forges a dead-member finding on a member that loads, a cap too high suppresses a real one (intent.md invariant 2).

edit.description: The constant and the cite that justifies it. `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` (62) sits under a doc (58-62) asserting 'the `at-import` grammar is recursion-capped at five hops (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-02)' — a number the live page does not say. temper's own indexing IS decidable on disk and needs no ruling: `live_members` (486-527) seeds every own-live member and runs `for _ in 0..MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` BFS rounds (513), one import edge per round, so the constant counts EDGES traversed from a live seed. What no reading of the disk settles is which edges the doc's 'four hops' counts — hence the park. Whatever the ruling: the cite is re-retrieved, re-dated, and re-worded to state the hop-counting it encodes, never left asserting a bare number its source does not carry. The two narration cites deferring to it — 384-385 ('as the format documents') and 483-484 ('the `at-import` recursion depth the format documents') — carry the same claim and move with it, as does the propagation comment at 508-511 ('capped at the format's import recursion depth').

`the_import_closure_is_hop_capped` (1238) already pins the boundary mechanically rather than in a comment — `r1..r5` rescued from a live seed, `r6` dead — so it encodes today's 5 in the one place a flip cannot silently miss, and it is the test that moves if the ruling is four. Its comment (1241-1243) spells 'the format's five-hop import recursion' and carries the same claim as the constant's cite; re-word it to the ruled reading rather than leaving the fixture narrating the retired number.

tests.asserts: the import closure rescues a chain exactly as deep as the ruled cap and no deeper — the boundary hop pinned at the ruled number, in the ruled counting

acceptance: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`, its doc cite, and the two narration cites that defer to it all agree with the live docs, and each states which hop-counting it encodes (spec-system.md, 'Form rules': an external fact carries its source and retrieval date where it is enforced, verified against current docs, never encoded from memory). The boundary hop stays pinned by a test at the ruled number. `cargo test` green.
notes: Park re-tested at 0c25b2c and holds — nothing ruled the hop semantics, and neither cited file has moved since 4a33911, so every line cite above still resolves (`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` reads 5 at graph.rs:62 under a cite claiming five). The entry stays parked: the constant may still be wrong, the one thing that keeps it out of the staleness-rider class. rescoped at 4a33911.

summary: The packaging remainder after the linux-x64+win32-x64 first cut: darwin binaries (Apple notarizing), the plugin/bundle + marketplace.json, and the binary↔npm version lockstep at the v0.1 tag

gate.reason: Human release actions: John's Apple notarizing for the darwin standalone binary + darwin optionalDependency package; the v0.1 launch tag that flips the binary's self-reported crate version (0.1.0) into lockstep with npm's 0.0.x. Channel 3 (plugin/bundle + marketplace.json) is a git repo + marketplace.json + free community web form (verified 2026-07-06, code.claude.com/docs) — no signing creds — but rides the same release-workflow build-out. release.yml (channel 2, linux-x64+win32-x64) + the SDK launcher/optionalDependencies + standalone tag assets shipped 07-11; the standalone-asset step is wired but unexercised until a tag fires.

edit.description: Extend the existing release.yml (linux-x64 + win32-x64 first cut, the darwin deferral stated at lines 7-9) with the darwin matrix targets (aarch64/x86_64-apple-darwin) + an Apple notarization step, and a channel-3 step producing/publishing the `temper bundle` output with its marketplace.json. Distinct from the standalone tag-asset step, already wired (unexercised until a tag).

acceptance: A stranger acquires temper on all three OSes without a Rust toolchain (darwin binary notarized + pinned), or via the plugin/bundle (marketplace.json); the binary self-reports 0.1.0 in lockstep with the v0.1 npm cut (distribution.md: prebuilt binaries install on all three OSes; lockstep engine pin).
notes: Release infra outside src/; cargo gates can't validate npm/bundle publish. Park re-tested on disk at 0c25b2c and holds on every clause: `git tag -l` carries only the four era tags and no version tag, crate 0.1.0 vs npm 0.0.7, and release.yml:7-9 still states the darwin + channel-3 deferral verbatim. scoped at b676656.
